key_insight: Compilation is not enough for Lean formalization: many type-checking statements are still semantically wrong, so faithfulness must be evaluated separately from validity.
engineering_relevance: For theorem-proving systems, this shows that pipeline design and benchmark reporting should distinguish proof success, statement correctness, and tool-augmented formalization quality.
method: Built a 400-entry graduate-level benchmark across analysis, topology, and algebra.
Evaluated outputs with Lean compilation, cross-model semantic judging, and human expert calibration.
Used a $2^3$ factorial study to isolate the effects of drafting, search, and elaboration feedback.
evidence_signal: A full tool-augmented agent achieved 89.5% compilation but only 60.5% consensus faithfulness, revealing a 29.0-point validity-versus-faithfulness gap.
watch_item: Cross-model semantic judgments are conservative and may miss some valid-but-subtle formalizations, so human audit coverage and calibration remain important.
paper_title: Beyond Compilation: Evaluating Faithful Natural-Language-to-Lean Statement Formalization
